coredata.dc:title: Tubular microreactors with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites supported on inner wall for photocatalytic applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Huadong
Sun, Hao
Sun, XiaoYu
Cheng, Jing liang
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a novel tubular microreactor immobilized a photocatalyst to overcome the limitations of low light utilization and the challenges associated with catalyst recovery in traditional photocatalysis reactors. The copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, S-CQDs)/bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) composites were successfully immobilized on the inner surface of the microreactor channel through the sol–gel process. The catalytic layer in the microreactor enlarged the specific surface area and facilitated the mass transfer. Meanwhile, it also greatly reduced the consumption of chemical supplies and eliminated the need for recycling the photocatalyst. The morphology and structural features of the catalytic layer were studied by characterization. Then the photocatalytic performance of the microreactor was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B. The photocatalytic kinetics of the photocatalytic reaction in the microreactor was 0.60925, which was 10.49 times greater than that in the macroscopic system. The results showed that the microreactor led to a significant improvement in degradation efficiency. The reproducibility experiments conducted on the microreactor system further demonstrate its excellent stability. In short, integrating the beneficial properties of microreactors with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits highly promising prospects of photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu
S-CQDs/BiVO4
Microreactor
Photocatalysis
Rhodamine B
Wastewater treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)∼(d) SEM images of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer.
SEM images of cross section of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer.
(a)∼(e) EDS element mapping of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer. (f) Content of each element.
XRD diffraction pattern of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (b) the pore size distribution of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer.
FT-IR patterns of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers.
Pseudo-first-order kinetics for Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic powder in a traditional stirred reactor.
Pseudo-first-order kinetics for Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer in tuber microreactors.
Stability of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic deposited microreactor in microfluidic devices.
The photocatalytic process in the microreactor.

head.ce:title: Tubular microreactors with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites supported on inner wall for photocatalytic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a novel tubular microreactor immobilized a photocatalyst to overcome the limitations of low light utilization and the challenges associated with catalyst recovery in traditional photocatalysis reactors. The copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, S-CQDs)/bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) composites were successfully immobilized on the inner surface of the microreactor channel through the sol–gel process. The catalytic layer in the microreactor enlarged the specific surface area and facilitated the mass transfer. Meanwhile, it also greatly reduced the consumption of chemical supplies and eliminated the need for recycling the photocatalyst. The morphology and structural features of the catalytic layer were studied by characterization. Then the photocatalytic performance of the microreactor was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B. The photocatalytic kinetics of the photocatalytic reaction in the microreactor was 0.60925, which was 10.49 times greater than that in the macroscopic system. The results showed that the microreactor led to a significant improvement in degradation efficiency. The reproducibility experiments conducted on the microreactor system further demonstrate its excellent stability. In short, integrating the beneficial properties of microreactors with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits highly promising prospects of photocatalytic applications.

This paper presents a novel tubular microreactor immobilized a photocatalyst to overcome the limitations of low light utilization and the challenges associated with catalyst recovery in traditional photocatalysis reactors. The copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, S-CQDs)/bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) composites were successfully immobilized on the inner surface of the microreactor channel through the sol–gel process. The catalytic layer in the microreactor enlarged the specific surface area and facilitated the mass transfer. Meanwhile, it also greatly reduced the consumption of chemical supplies and eliminated the need for recycling the photocatalyst. The morphology and structural features of the catalytic layer were studied by characterization. Then the photocatalytic performance of the microreactor was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B. The photocatalytic kinetics of the photocatalytic reaction in the microreactor was 0.60925, which was 10.49 times greater than that in the macroscopic system. The results showed that the microreactor led to a significant improvement in degradation efficiency. The reproducibility experiments conducted on the microreactor system further demonstrate its excellent stability. In short, integrating the beneficial properties of microreactors with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits highly promising prospects of photocatalytic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of the manufacturing industry, the amount of industrial wastewater containing massive organic pollutants and antibiotics has increased significantly, causing great damage to the ecological environment, and seriously threatening the life and health of human beings and aquatic organisms[1–3]. Among them, Rhodamine B is one of the most common wastewater pollutants[4]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find an efficient method to eliminate wastewater pollutants[5–7]. The current methods for degrading pollutants mainly include adsorption, biological treatment, coagulation, the thin-film method, and advanced oxidation[8–11]. Outstandingly, photocatalysis in the advanced oxidation method attracts great attention[12–14]. Photocatalytic oxidation reaction uses semiconductor materials as catalysts, uses light as energy, excites the generation of photogenerated electrons and holes under light, combines with oxygen and other oxidizing agents, and degrades the organic matter into carbon dioxide and water, which is a green and efficient method with the concept of sustainable development[15–17]. Among them, BiVO4 is considered a green and safe photocatalyst[18–20]. However, photocatalysis faces some limitations, low mass transfer efficiency, and low photon transmission efficiency[21,22]. The traditional photocatalytic process is carried out by dispersing the catalyst powder in an intermittent stirred reactor, which inevitably leads to catalyst agglomeration and lower photon transfer efficiency[23–25]. Moreover, the catalyst in the reactor needs to be filtered and separated after each reaction, which is not only cumbersome but also a consumption of chemicals[26–28]. Then, microreactors have a large specific surface area, high mass transfer efficiency and precise flow rate control, which proves to be very effective for catalytic reactions[29].

In recent years, microreactors have developed rapidly and are widely used in various industries[30–33]. The microreactor, microchannel reactor, is a kind of microreactor with a channel size of less than 1 mm. The smaller internal size presents a lot of advantages that are different from those of conventional reactors[34]. For example, microreactors have a large specific surface area, a short molecular diffusion length, and a high reaction efficiency, which can effectively promote the mass transfer effect, significantly enhance the catalytic reaction, and improve the conversion rate[35–37]. In addition, traditional reactors are more complex and costly due to the more complex system units, while microreactors, as independent reaction systems, have great advantages in integration[38]. In industrial production, hundreds of microchannels can be included in one microreactor, which can very effectively save time and cost and improve the reaction efficiency[39]. Therefore, in the past decades, microreactors have been introduced into various application fields, including fine chemical synthesis, nanomaterials preparation, biochemical analysis, energy conversion, utilization, etc., and have shown good application potential in these fields[40]. In photocatalytic reactions, the advantages of microreactors, large specific surface area and precise control of fluid flow rate, can greatly improve the mass transfer efficiency, photon utilization and catalyst utilization[41]. Compared with the traditional powder catalyst, the catalyst is difficult to separate, easy to agglomerate, and high cost, the use of catalyst-loaded microreactors can improve the efficiency of the light and have a larger area of the light so that the photons can quickly contact the surface of the catalyst, reduce the product sub-catalyst separation process[42–44]. Therefore, the catalyst-loaded microreactors carry out continuous photocatalysis for the treatment of wastewater and are green and economical[45]. The biggest disadvantage of the microstructure of the microreactor is that solid materials cannot pass through the microchannels, and the microchannels are very easy to be clogged, resulting in the production not being able to run continuously, if many solids are produced in the reaction[46–48]. Therefore, the loading of the catalyst is the most important in the application of the microreactor in photocatalysis[49]. Common catalyst loading methods include the sol–gel method, as-reduction method, and layer-by-layer self-assembly method[50–52]. The Layer-by-layer self-assembly technique mainly involves the preparation of a polymerized electrolyte layer in the form of layer-by-layer adsorption by electrostatic forces, and then it is used to adsorb the precursor solution and carry out in-situ reduction. This method is mild and has higher catalytic activity, but it is not universally applicable due to its poor stability and high requirements for experimental materials. Outstandingly, the catalytic layer prepared by the sol–gel method has the advantages of low synthesis temperature, strong sintering resistance, good catalytic activity, etc. [53–56]. We use bismuth vanadate as the photocatalytic material. We have already improved the photocatalytic performance of bismuth vanadate by doping carbon quantum dots to inhibition the rapid compounding of photogenerated electrons in previous work[57]. Now we try to further improve its photocatalytic efficiency by loading the copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots/ bismuth vanadate composites into the microreactor.

In this paper, we explore the feasibility of combining Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites with microreactors to create a more efficient catalytic system. The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites were successfully loaded onto the inner walls of microchannels through the sol–gel method. Compared with the conventional batch reactor, the catalyst-loaded tubular microchannel reactor has higher mass transfer efficiency, more precise flow control, reduces catalyst waste, eliminates the difficulty of catalyst recycling, and enhances the catalytic performance of the catalyst. The prepared microchannel reactor was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), BET Specific Surface Area Tester (BET), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) to observe the loading of the catalyst on the inner wall of the microchannel. The photocatalytic performance of the microreactor was analyzed by the degradation of Rhodamine B. The degradation was investigated at different flow rates, light intensities, and pollutant concentrations. Moreover, the kinetic analysis of the degradation process and the reproducibility experiments were conducted to investigate the reaction rate and stability of microreactors. This work could reveal a novel method to improve the efficiency for photocatalysis.

The microchannel uses polyethylene Tygon hoses with a diameter of 0.51 mm and a length of 10 cm, purchased from Saint Gobain Group. L-ascorbic acid, Ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3,99.5 %), Bismuth solution (Bi (NO3)3·5H2O,99 %), Rhodamine B (RHB), Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·.5H2O,99 %), ethanol, Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), Dopamine hydrochloride (98 %), Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris, >99.8 %) purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents are analytical pure and can be used without further purification. All aqueous solutions are pure water.

The preparation of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite materials has been described in our previous work[57]. A brief introduction: First, copper and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized using ascorbic acid and copper sulfate pentahydrate. Then, bismuth vanadate was synthesized using bismuth nitrate pentahydrate and ammonium metavanadate. Then, Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite materials were prepared by room temperature impregnation method.

Conventional photocatalytic degradation is generally carried out in beaker mode, where the catalyst is mixed with the pollutant in the form of powder, which leads to some problems such as uneven light distribution, low overall efficiency, and difficulty in separating the catalyst[58–60]. We fix the catalyst composites to the inner wall of the microchannels in the form of gels to solve these problems, improve catalyst utilization, and make full use of light[61]. At the same time, reflective white paper was laid on the bottom to further utilize the reflected light and improve the light collection capacity of the microreactor. The solid-loaded catalyst structure enlarges the specific surface area of the photocatalyst and exposes more active sites to the reactants, which can greatly improve the mass transfer capacity and enhance the photocatalytic performance[62,63]. The specific operation steps are as follows:
(1)
Inject denoised water and ethanol into microchannels repeatedly through the micro syringe pump, to clean the microreactor, then, then dry it at 70 degrees for 4 h.
(2)
Weigh 2 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), pour it into 80 ml of deionized water and mix it well, and then heat the solution to 95 °C with continuous stirring until the polyvinyl alcohol is completely dissolved.
(3)
Weigh 20 mg of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite powder, pour it into 16 ml of well-mixed polyvinyl alcohol aqueous solution, and then heat the solution to 60 °C with continuous stirring for 6 h until the catalyst powder is completely dissolved, to make a catalyst sol.
(4)
Weigh 30 mg Tris powder, and 50 mg dopamine hydrochloride powder, pour into 30 ml deionized water, and shake ultrasonically until completely dissolved. Pass the configured dopamine solution continuously into the microchannel through the micro syringe pump at a flow rate of 2 μl /min for 3 h, and then rinse off the unadhered dopamine coating with deionized water. Finally, pass into the air to purge the excess water and dry it for 8 h under the condition of 70 °C.
(5)
Inject Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite sol into the pretreated microchannels through the syringe pump at a flow rate of 2 μl/min for 2 h, and pass air to blow out the excess Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite sol after standing for 30 min. The process of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite sol feeding and blowing was repeated three times to obtain enough catalytic layer on the inner wall of the microchannels. Finally, it was dried at 70 °C for 12 h to produce a solid catalytic layer.

The microreactor platform mainly consists of micro syringe pumps, hoses, xenon lamps, and testing instruments in Fig. 1 . The micro syringe pump was from Lange Constant Flow Pumps Ltd. The microchannels were made of polyethylene Tygon tubing with a diameter of 510 um and a length of 10 cm. Tygon tubing has excellent properties for precision injections. It is exceptionally flexible, non-toxic, odorless, non-pyrogenic, biocompatible, completely transparent, chemically stable of the product stability, corrosion-resistant, and has a very low water absorption rate. The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites were used as photocatalysts. A stainless-steel support was used to fix the microchannels, and reflective white paper was laid on the support to further utilize the reflected light and improve the light collection capacity of the microreactor. The light was provided by a xenon lamp light source (CEL-HXF300), which was aimed at the microchannels with a distance of 10 cm from the UV light to the microchannels. The reacted liquids were detected using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (FluoroMax-4).

The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer was characterized by SEM, EDS, and XRD. The SEM images were used to observe the microstructure of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer deposited on the inner wall of the microreactor channel by ZEISS Gemini SEM 300, Germany, at 200 KV. Cut the microchannel along the inner diameter with a small knife to capture the inner wall and cross-section of the microchannel. The elemental species and content of the materials were analyzed by the EDS spectrometer (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy). The XRD patterns were tested using an X-ray diffractometer, model Rigaku Smart Lab-SE, Japan, with a range of 10–80° and a sweep speed of 2°/min and the source of light was Cu-K α-rays, the tube voltage is 40 kV and current is 40 mA. The FTIR analysis was done to understand the components and surface groups of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers on an FTIR spectrophotometer. The specific surface area and pore diameters (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460) was analyzed via N2 adsorption–desorption.

Rhodamine B was studied as a pollutant since it is a common residue in industrial wastewater. The photocatalytic performance of the microreactor was investigated by determining the degradation of Rhodamine B in the microreactor at ambient temperature. The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composite materials were used as photocatalysts for the experiments. The catalyst was solidified on the inner wall of the microreactor channel by the sol–gel method. The initial concentration of Rhodamine B solution at the inlet was 2 mg/l. A 300 W xenon lamp was used to simulate the sunlight irradiation to the microreactor, and the Rhodamine B solution was injected into the microreactor by a precision syringe pump at a flow rate ranging from 5 to 20 μl/min, with the effective residence times of 1 min, 2 min, 3 min, 4 min, and 5 min. The micro syringe pump operated for 1 h in dark conditions to make the catalyst layer reach the adsorption and resolution equilibrium, and the microreactor reaches a stable state. The experiment was started after the light emission of the lamp was stabilized, the reaction products were collected from the outlet at different time intervals, and the photocatalytic degraded solution was collected in a brown plastic vial to avoid the influence of light. To avoid experimental errors caused by chance factors, each experiment was repeated three times. Samples were taken for analysis by UV–visible spectrophotometer to measure the absorbance at the wavelength of 554 nm. According to the Beer-Lambert law, the absorbance is directly proportional to the concentration, therefore, the conversion rate can be calculated with the following equation:
X =
C 0
-
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
=
A 0
-
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %
where X is the conversion rate of Rhodamine B in the reaction, C0 and Ct are the concentrations at moment 0 and t, and A0 and At are the corresponding absorbance values.

We have already described the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites in the previous paper[57], where it was concluded from characterization analysis that Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites were successfully synthesize. Now, we mainly went to analyze the loading on the microchannels of the microreactor. The loading thickness and microscopic morphology of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer were characterized by scanning electron microscopy. As shown in the Fig. 2 (a)∼(d), the top view of the surface of the inner wall of the microchannel shows the morphological characteristics of the catalyst, which is uniformly distributed on the wall of the tube, and its shape is approximately petal-like. The surface is smooth, and the petal-like shape has a larger surface area used to receive the light, which leads to the generation of more photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increases the contact area of the catalyst with Rhodamine B. Comparing the SEM images of the catalyst powder, the sol makes the catalyst more dispersed and forms a smoother, flatter surface.

In Fig. 3 , the cross-sectional view of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer can indicate that the inner wall of the microchannel is loaded with a uniformly distributed layer of catalyst, and the average thickness of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer is calculated to be about 6.96 um.

In Fig. 4 (a)∼(e), the energy dispersive spectroscopy analysis of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers shows that elements Bi, V, O, and C are indeed present in the catalytic layers loaded on the microreactor and the composite is dominated by bismuth vanadate. The mapping also further proves the uniformity of the catalysts loaded on the microreactor channels. Fig. 4(f) illustrates the percentage of each major element in each catalytic layer, with 16 %, 29 %, 44 %, and 9 % for Bi, V, O, and C, respectively.

The phase structure of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers was analyzed by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy. The XRD pattern of the obtained catalytic layers is shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks at 18.5°, 28.9°, 30.5°, and 34.5° represent the (0 1 1), (1 2 1), (0 4 0), and (2 0 0) crystal planes. Respectively, it is almost consistent with the crystal structure of the monoclinic phase BiVO4 (JCPDS14-0688), which is in accordance with the monoclinic crystal structure of bismuth vanadate[64–66]. This also proves that the sol–gel treatment did not affect the crystal structure of the catalyst.

The BET analysis was used to investigate critical catalyst characteristics such surface area and pore diameters, as shown in Fig. 6 . The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer exhibit a typical type IV isotherm in Fig. 6(a) [67,68]. The BET study results show a BET surface area is 0.1924 m2/g. The pore size distribution of the catalytic layer is shown in Fig. 6(b), which indicates that the synthesized material is mesoporous.

The FTIR analysis was done to understand the components and surface groups of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers, as shown in Fig. 7 . The 706 cm−1 peak and 833 cm−1 peak show that the (V–O–V) two vanadium atoms attached to the central oxygen atom and exhibit antisymmetric stretching modes[69]. Bi–O bending vibration can be observed below 700 cm−1, while Bi–O asymmetric vibration appeared at 1359 cm−1 [70]. Moreover, a vibration mode peak of V(Bi–O–Bi) is also observed at 1129.79 cm−1 [71]. The Infrared bands from 1300 to 1600 cm−1 and 3300 to 3600 cm−1 are the O–H bond adsorbed on the surface of the sample[72]. Besides, the two characteristic peaks at 1395 cm−1 and 1644 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O due to the adsorption of H2O and CO2 on the sample surface under air atmosphere[73].

The photocatalytic performance of the device can be obtained from the degradation experiment of Rhodamine B in the microreactor with loaded Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalyst. The initial concentration of Rhodamine B solution is 2 mg/l, and the flow rate of the pump is 5 μl/l. The Rhodamine B solution was nearly completely degraded within 5 min. The degradation efficiency was much higher than that of the conventional stirred reactor. It was attributed to the excellent performance of the microreactor that the high specific surface area increased the active surface sites of the reactor, faster diffusion, more uniform light irradiation, enhanced mass transfer, and fixed catalysts simultaneously improved light harvesting capacity. To further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the device, the degradation effect of the device was tested under different flow rates, light intensities, and initial Rhodamine B concentrations. In addition, there were no changes in the Rhodamine B concentration in the microreactor without a loaded catalyst.

The flow rates were set from, 5 to 20 μl/min, and the corresponding effective residence times were 1 min, 2 min, 3 min, 4 min, and 5 min to test the degradation effect. Respectively, as is shown in Fig. 8 , extending the residence time can improve the degradation efficiency of the microreactor. The extension of the residence time of the reactants in the microreactors resulted in a more effective contact between the Rhodamine B molecules and the photocatalysts[74]. In addition, more residence time reduces the load on the wastewater treatment system and contributes to the degradation efficiency.

The effect of light intensity on photocatalytic degradation was investigated. The xenon lamp‘s operating voltage is 14 V. The intensity of light was set to 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.5, and 5.1 mW/cm2. It can be seen from the Fig. 9 that the degradation efficiency increased with the increase of light intensity at the same pollutant concentration and flow rate. The degradation efficiency increased from 50 % to 86 % as the intensity of light changes from 2.7 mW/cm2 to 5.1 mW/cm2. When the light intensity is increased, the photon energy is absorbed by the material, which causes significant changes in the internal energy band structure of the material. Under weak light conditions, the electrons in the valence band of the material absorb photon energy and then jump to the conduction band in a relatively limited process, and the distribution of electronic states in the energy band is relatively sparse. As the light intensity increases, more photons are absorbed and a large number of electrons in the valence band are excited to the conduction band, resulting in a rapid increase in the concentration of electrons in the conduction band, while a large number of holes are generated in the valence band. This change in the energy band structure leads to the formation of a stronger internal electric field inside the material, and the separation efficiency of electrons and holes is dramatically improved, reducing the chances of their compounding, which will lead to the degradation of more Rhodamine B[75]. At the same time, as the light intensity increases, more incident light will also penetrate the surface of the microreactor to reach the catalytic layer at the bottom, thus promoting the photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

Under the same flow rate and light intensity, the degradation effect was tested under the conditions of Rhodamine B concentration of 1, 2, 3, and 4 mg/l. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 10 . With the increase of Rhodamine B concentration, the degradation efficiency gradually decreased, which is because under the condition of constant catalyst loading, the active substances generated on photocatalytic conditions are limited and cannot meet the requirements of degradation of pollutants at higher concentrations under the condition of constant catalyst loading, and only the pollutants adsorbed on the surface of the catalytic layer may obtain electrons and holes, and thus be degraded.

We carried out a kinetic analysis of the reaction process and discussed the reaction rate constants to gain a deeper understanding of the photocatalytic performance of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 loaded microreactor. It can be concluded that the degradation reaction was found to be a pseudo-first-order reaction by analyzing the experimental data, which was fitted to the experimental data to obtain the value of the reaction rate constant. The measurement of the reaction rate is generally calculated by the change in concentration of the pollutant at different time points. The integral and differential methods are the two main methods used to process the data when studying kinetics. The formula for calculating the rate constant can be expressed as the following equation:
V = -
d C t
dt
= K t
ln
Ct
Co
= K t
ln
Co
Co - X
= K t
where C0 and Ct are the concentrations at time 0 and time t, respectively, K is the reaction rate constant, and t is the residence time.

The Fig. 11 shows a photocatalytic experiment carried out in a conventional reactor, where Rhodamine B pollutant was placed in a beaker, catalyst powder was added, and the reaction was carried out under xenon lamp irradiation. The Fig. 12 shows the photocatalytic experiments carried out on the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalyst-loaded microreactor in this paper, where the catalyst was loaded onto the inner wall of the microchannel, and Rhodamine B was injected into the microchannel using the micro syringe pump, and the experiments were carried out under xenon lamp irradiation.

From the calculated reaction rate constants, it can be concluded that the reaction rate constant of the catalyst-loaded microreactor increased from 0.05806 to 0.60925 compared to the conventional reactor, which is magnified by ten times, and the degradation efficiency was greatly improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and Reusability of the microreactor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layer is crucial for the application of microreactors in photocatalysis. We investigated the variation of the degradation effect of the microreactors under continuous operation conditions to test the stability of the device. The stability tests were performed on the catalyst-loaded microreactors at a flow rate of 5 μl/min. The reactive pollutants were collected from the device at regular intervals for testing for 24 h of continuous operation. The results of the tests from Fig. 13 indicated that the conversion rate was almost constant. In addition, there were no catalyst-loaded detachments collected at the channel outlet, which confirms the stability of the inner wall solidly loaded catalyst layer on the microreactor under mild reaction conditions.

When the microreactor is applied to photocatalysis, the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites can receive more uniform light and can better utilize the photon energy. Meanwhile, a large amount of light can be absorbed in a fixed, high specific surface area photoreactive layer and kept in a very small volume, which can be efficiently coupled to the light source. The photocatalytic reactions occurring in the tubular microreactor are mainly divided into several processes, such as fluid flow, surface adsorption, catalytic degradation under light, product desorption and mass diffusion, as shown in Fig. 14 . Firstly, Rhodamine B molecules were pumped into the microreactor channel, and those molecules close to the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalytic layers were first adsorbed. Then the catalyst excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, generating more holes and photogenerated under the excitation of light. The photogenerated electrons react with the oxygen in the water to produce superoxide radicals. Moreover, the Rhodamine B molecules undergo a redox reaction in the presence of holes and superoxide radicals and is decomposed into CO2 and H2O. The depletion of rhodamine B molecules leads to a decrease in the concentration of reactants in the vicinity of the catalytic layer. Then, rhodamine B molecules keep moving towards the catalytic layer driven by the concentration gradient. As a result, the concentration in the microreactor starts to decrease and the process is repeated continuously in the microreactor. The photocatalytic reaction ends after all reactants have been degraded and flowed out of the microreactor.

Microreactors are potentially ideal photocatalytic platforms due to their excellent properties, such as large specific surface area, high mass transfer efficiency, and precise flow rate control. In this paper, we successfully prepared a tubular microreactor with Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 catalyst loaded onto the inner wall of the microchannels. The characterization results showed that a thick catalytic layer, with an average thickness of 6.96 um, was formed on the inner wall of the microchannel. It can be concluded from the Rhodamine B degradation experiments that a 2 mg/l solution of Rhodamine B was completely degraded in 5 min. The reaction rate constant is 0.60925, which is 10.49 times higher than that of the conventional reactor. This was attributed to the efficient mixing of reactant in the microreactor, increased specific surface area, enhanced mass transfer, and more uniform light distribution. The solid-loaded catalyst in the microreactor also reduces the consumption of catalytic chemicals and facilitates the catalyst recycling. It can be concluded through repeated experiments that it has high stability, and the catalytic layer is not prone to detachment. As an independent reaction system, the microreactor has a significant advantage in integration. It has a wide range of application prospects for the removal of organic pollutants in industrial wastewater.
There is no human or animal study in this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the author(s) have not used the AI and AI assisted technologies.

Huadong Liu: Investigation, Conceptualization. Hao Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. XiaoYu Sun: Validation. Jing liang Cheng: Validation.
There is no funding received in the study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Foundation of He'nan Educational Committee (24A470010).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, L.; Yue, X.; Chang, Y.; Wang, K.; Zhang, J.; Sun, J.; Wei, Z.; Kowalska, E., Photocatalytic Degradation of Tetracycline under Visible Light Irradiation on BiVO4 Microballs Modified with Noble Metals. Catalysts 2022, 12 (11).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elevating the photocatalytic performance of BiOCl by promoting light utilization: from Co doping to the microreactor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Y.; Wang, R.; Gao, H.; Huang, H.; Dong, R.; Lu, C.; Kou, J., Elevating the Photocatalytic Performance of BiOCl by Promoting Light Utilization: From Co doping to the Microreactor. The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 2024, 15 (5), 1412-1419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-film optofluidic microreactor with enhanced light-harvesting for photocatalytic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, L.; Tang, D.; Song, Y.; Jiang, B., Dual-film optofluidic microreactor with enhanced light-harvesting for photocatalytic applications. Chemical Engineering Journal 2018, 339, 71-77.
